text.title: Prototype Process and Fidelity
text.summary: Five-step prototyping process with fidelity decision matrix and handoff artifact checklist.

section.title: Fidelity Decision Matrix

rule.statement: Choose fidelity based on what you need to learn, not on how much time you have.
rule.rationale: Fidelity that exceeds learning needs wastes design time and creates false attachment to visuals before validation.

section.p: Low fidelity (paper, basic wireframes) → concept testing, hours to create. Medium fidelity (clickable screens, key interactions) → flow testing, days to create. High fidelity (near-final, animated) → experience testing, weeks to create. Code prototype (HTML/CSS/React) → technical feasibility, engineering time.

section.title: Five-Step Process

rule.statement: Write learning objectives before selecting tools or fidelity level.
rule.rationale: Objectives determine scope; scope determines fidelity; fidelity determines tool. Reversing this order produces over-built prototypes.

section.p: Step 1: Define objectives — state each as a testable question (e.g., "Can users complete checkout without help?").
Step 2: Determine fidelity — use the matrix above. When in doubt, start lower.
Step 3: Create prototype — include key screens, critical interactions, realistic content, and a clear user path. Prototype the riskiest assumption first.
Step 4: Test with users — define 3-5 tasks, observe without intervening, note failures and confusion.
Step 5: Iterate — fix major issues, re-test if critical flows changed, document decisions, hand off.

rule.statement: Limit moderated test tasks to 3-5 per session.
rule.rationale: More tasks produce fatigue and diluted observations, not more insight.

section.title: Prototype Types
section.p: Paper — sketches and paper screens; fast, cheap, anyone can facilitate. Clickable (Figma hotspots) — linked screens; quick to build, shareable, testable. Interactive (Figma/Framer/ProtoPie) — animations and transitions; realistic but time-intensive. Code (React/HTML) — technical validation; highest fidelity, real implementation constraints.

example.note: Clickable Figma prototype for checkout flow validation: create screen designs, add hotspots, link screens, define transitions. Tests flow comprehension without investing in animations.
Building a ProtoPie interactive prototype with full animations before any user has seen the basic flow. Wastes days on polish that testing may invalidate entirely.

section.title: Handoff Artifacts
section.p: When handing off to development, include: prototype link (interactive reference), screen specs (measurements), interaction specs (behavior definitions), assets (icons/images), component library reference, and documented edge cases (error states, empty states).

rule.statement: Document what was NOT prototyped and why.
rule.rationale: Prevents stakeholder scope creep during handoff; sets correct expectations for what remains unvalidated.
